sentences.text: Yes, there were instruments of torture in every camp, then in-- some of these were in special places, some of these were-- that had been moved from their original location to where they could be seen by troops because Buchenwald was one of the camps that Eisenhower said, I want to troops to go through this camp.
So a lot of American soldiers went through the camp after we left.
One of the most unusual things-- and I couldn't figure this out till someone explained it to me-- was what they called the swing.
And what it was, it was a, a, a round pole, probably made out of some very strong wood, probably this, this thick.
And it was built like a swing, you know, with-- like you could hook it to a swing, to a hook, and if it wasn't a round pole, if it had been flattened, it would be a little seat for a kid's swing.
But this pole was round, and the way it worked was-- and I never would have figured this out.
They, they tied the person's hands behind his back.
And they put this pole, which has chains on it, behind his knees, and they tie his knees-- his feet to his back.
In other words, his, his, his hands and his feet are behind his back.
He's hanging upside down with this pole, which is the seat for this-- would have been the seat for the swing if it was a swing.
And that's what they called it.
And, and it's hooked onto a swivel, a chain, kind of like a tripod with a chain.
And then they have-- and it-- and it swivels.
So you could spin the guy like this.
And what these-- what they did was they would have clubs, and they would beat him on the buttocks and legs with these clubs and spin him around on this swing, both directions.
You know, they'd-- there would be a circle of SS guys around him, and as they-- as his buttocks went by, they'd hit him again and the momentum to keep him moving.
Then they would stop, and they'd do it the other way.
Now-- and-- now this is the way they explained this to me.
Very few men survived that because they were hitting the kidneys and, and other places as opposed to-- the idea was to hit him on the buttocks and on the top of their thighs.
It's very, very painful.
And to get them to confess or punish them for something.
They didn't have to have a reason.
They just punished them.
That was probably the weirdest thing that I saw.
And another one which was very common, especially at Dachau, well, was making the inmates beat each other with, with sticks and with whips.
That was this-- that was kind of like a-- that was a form of recreation for the SS was to get the weaker inmates to be beaten by the stronger inmates or vice versa.
And if they refused to do it, they were beaten to death by the SS.
Well, one of the most-- and here, again, I don't understand how anybody could tolerate this-- was, was be like a four by four post stuck in the ground.

And they-- so they would tie a person's hands behind their back, tie their legs together behind their back, and it had a pulley like a, like a water-- like a well would have a pulley to bring up the bucket.
And they would pull this rope with this man-- now this is by his arms.
I don't want to-- his, his arms would be back here, and then it would be connected to this rope, which they would pull the pulley.
Would pull his arms clear out of the sockets usually, and pull him up six or seven feet on this post and just let him hang there five, six, eight hours.
Never give him any water.
And there'd be, you know, 10 or 15 posts like that.
And if any infraction of any kind, or just because I decided, OK, put him up on the post.
That's how it was.
Total, absolute power.
Total absolute helplessness.
And this is when you see-- when you-- when you have time to contemplate this or talk about this with someone else, that's when you get angry.
That's when you say, you know-- and you use bad language.
We got to get back.
You know, God damn it, if we get a chance-- if we get back in combat-- see, now we're still talking about April.
We've didn't know when the war was-- we wanted to get back so bad.
We remembered, a couple of us that, that were in that shoot-- that turkey shoot in, in Belgium, we wanted to do that so bad, you know?
Remember I told you I had felt a little bit guilty about this thing, and it was kind of an abstract thing?
I relived that over and over, and it just got off on it.
It made me feel good.
God damn, I wish I had a chance to do that a whole bunch of times.
That's how-- you know-- just kill those bastards for what they had done to these people.
And that was very difficult, but that feeling was so intense that some of us-- because we, we just-- we just have to shut up and-- a few guys cracked up.
I, I only cracked up once, but it was in, in Dachau.
So we went back to Frankfurt.
Took our guys back to Frankfurt.
Can we go back just a little about the--
I beg your pardon?
Sure.
Sorry.
Oh, burial.
And the people.
This was something that was really kind of unusual.

Before they buried these stacks of peo-- of inmates, they brought them-- they went into the town and they told the mayor that they wanted all the citizens of, of Weimar to come to the camp in their Sunday best, and they would be escorted by American guards.
And they were going to be taken on a tour of the camp.
Now, I didn't know any of these men involved that did this, but I was told that they were instructed to tell the mayor, to ask, did you-- did you people know about this out there?
And of course, nobody said they knew.
Well, some-- we saw some men marching.
Hell, the roads, you know, you could throw a rock and hit the camp from some of those farms.
What about the smell?
Didn't they say they had that smell?
Oh yeah, and that's something we all wondered.
We all asked.
We all p-- who you-- I mean, I didn't get a chance to go up to somebody and bust them in the mouth and say, you're a lying son of a bitch.
I could smell that from the airplane, which was a mile away, and your house is only 500 yards away.
Come on.
But some of these guys got to do that.
And I didn't, and probably I'm glad-- I'm glad I didn't just-- I think it would've been real easy to commit mayhem.
It would have been real easy to, to do things that you shouldn't do, which would have lowered us to the same level of these people.
The-- they went into Weimar.
They brought out men, women, and children all dressed in their Sunday finest.
They took them through the entire tour, just like I had been through, through the stacks of the bodies, into the-- into the organ room, into the crematoriums with the bodies still inside the ovens.
To-- and they had taken-- there were-- there were no bodies laying around like when we first got there.
They had cleaned up that part.
But there were stacks of bodies, of thousands of bodies, 1,000, 1,500, 2,000.
I don't have any idea.
And the smell was just as bad as it was, if not worse.

They went through the-- which was-- which is an isolation ward, an isolation section where these men that I told you about that-- where, where the excrement was so thick on the floor that you, you, you couldn't avoid stepping in it.
And, and these people were still alive, and they couldn't get away, you know?
I mean, they could not-- they were ex-- they were having bowel movements on each other, and they couldn't do-- they couldn't do-- they couldn't walk away from it.
And I can't imagine anything worse than that except physical death maybe, I don't know, torture or something, to be that helpless.

And, and to be-- to be the victim as well as to be both the person who was sick enough to do that and the person who was too sick to get away were equally-- that must have been equally mortified by the fact that they couldn't control what was happening at all.
It just happened.
And there's probably 200 men in this-- in this section of barracks.
OK, let me get finished with the people because that was-- was really interesting.
They-- and I didn't participate in any of that.
But they took them all through the camp, and they-- and they walked them back, marched-- escorted with soldiers with bayonets on them.
Because the, the, the Americans were never-- I never saw any-- well, let me start-- I'm getting ahead of myself.
There was tremendous rage, but there was controlled rage.
Nobody yelled or spit on these-- even the inmates, these civilians, they just let them go by.
Nobody said anything to them.
When they were-- they wal-- they escorted back down the road toward Weimar, and there was a lieutenant that was in charge, a young infantry lieutenant.
Somebody laughed, a woman or some women or some girls.
I don't know.
I didn't hear this.
But here they come again.
He turned them around when he heard this laughter, and said, we're going to go back through that camp.
And they took them back, and they took another tour of the same damn thing they had been through before with these soldiers with the bayonets.
And they left the camp, and then there was no laughing on the second.
Now, I saw this, and Harry Herder-- the man that's on the internet at this Library of the Holocaust-- tells about this.
And he was there before I got there.
He was one of the tankers that went through the barbed wire and knocked the barbed wire down.
OK.
The engineers were brought in, and, they-- and-- and we left before they were finished.
And they took an area up on a hill, and it was whatever they do to make it a sacred place.
They made it a sacred place, the rabbis and the priests and whoever.
I don't know.
I don't remember.
A wonderful man.
I'd give anything to meet him.
I have never met a rabbi yet that I didn't just fall in love with.
No, no, no.
He was an inmate.
He was an inmate.
Oh yeah.
Yeah, he's the one that was a-- was a cigarette-- I, I wish the guy had quit.
I had-- I had cigarettes coming out my ears.
I probably had 10 or 15 cartons of cigarettes when I got there because we used-- that was worth money, you know?
You could barter cigarettes for anything in, in Europe.
Oh, I'm sorry.
They-- OK, they consecrated the ground, and they brought in engineers.
And the engineers, it was almost like it was a-- it was-- instead of just digging ditches, like ordinarily they would do this, they were very methodical, very careful.
And they were-- and they cleaned up the bodies, the medics did, and put them in there in nice, neat head to head to toe rows in the ground, in about seven or eight graves.
And they were in the process of doing that when we flew back to Fr-- to Frankfurt.
And that-- that's-- the last memory I have of Buchenwald was the feeling I had of almost reverence on the part of the men who were doing this job.
They, they would put the bodies in and carry them.
Then other inmates would-- they would carry them like babies.
And these people had been dead, some of them, for some period of time.
No dragging permitted.
By Americans.
Yeah, and the inmates.
Inmates were involved.
Mass graves, yep.
But it wasn't just like a ditch.
At Bergen-Belsen, it was just ditches that were dug with, with skip loaders and bulldozers.
And at, at, at Mauth-- ugh, Mauthausen-- at Buchenwald, it was done with care and like it was almost like a bunch of funerals.
It was a-- it was a big funeral is what it was.
But I didn't get to see it all.
I-- [SIGHS] Well, we were dusted as we were-- all the airplanes and passengers were dusted.
And, and I-- we smoked.
I remember the officer that was with me was-- I think he was a medic, a colonel, a surgeon.
And he smoked.
Well, everybody smoked.
And he said something like, you know, it's just reminded him of something out of Shakespeare of man's inhumanity to man.
It's just-- you know, it's something that you just can't believe that you've seen.
He said, I'm not going to be able to explain this to anybody, something like that.
And we're puffing on our Lucky's or whatever, and I said something like, I'll n-- the smell is all over me.
I got that smell.
I could smell it in my hair, and I've been taking showers and everything, using s-- GI soap, and then-- I just had that smell.
And it wouldn't go away.
That, well-- that's a whole different story.
So that was the end of-- pretty much.
There are probably other things, but they're not that significant.
And we're-- there's so much more.
I'm afraid we're not going to get to do to this all.
But, OK, we got back to Frankfurt.
We're debriefed by a G2.
We told them the same thing, I guess, that everybody else told them.
Then I'm-- but I'm sure that there was some serious interrogation on the part of the officers, the high-ranking officers.
And then, of course, they were passing on things that needed to be done.
Get some infantry up there.
Get some medics up there.
They ended up with a full field hospital in about 48 hours at Buchenwald.
No, we were all debriefed.
Yeah, individually and collectively.
And the individual debriefing was basically, what did you see?
And, and know what they were doing was just making sure that everybody was on the same wavelength.
There were some people that didn't see the organ room, for example.
They just didn't.
Or they didn't go into the, the house of the-- commandant's house.
The lamps, there were three, as I told you before.
According to Harry Herder, who was there and went in on that day, was lib-- was a liberator, per se.
I don't consider myself a liberator, per se.
I did not liberate, liberate anybody per se, you know?
A liberator, according to Shoah, is someone who was there 48 hours or something like that after.
But as far as fighting my way in, like some of these guys did, I didn't do that.
So what happened after that when you got debriefed?
The next place we went was Dachau.
We went-- we were told to get-- I'll try to make this briefer.
This with the 29th of April, 1945.
We were-- we're-- our destination was Munich.
Left Frankfurt early in the morning and stopped and refueled someplace along the way-- I don't remember where-- which was not unusual.
Used to go to the bathroom and maybe get a sandwich or something and refuel.
And I got to Munich about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 11:30, 11:00, 11:30, 12:00, well, before noon.
And we were to be met by elements of the infantry units that-- and the tank units that had-- that were fighting in the area.
Radioed and were told not to try to land in Munich.
There was still-- there was still fighting going on.
Turn around, go back to Frankfurt.
You can't land around here.
Well, the flight leader said, we're, we're-- our destination is Dachau.
Can we get to Dachau?
Is-- and these guys talked on the radio, and we could hear them.
And, and he mentioned some unit that was in Dachau that was-- that was at the gates of Dachau.
They were-- they were in the camp, but the camp was out of control.

And, and-- but there were-- there were troops there, so we could-- we could communicate with the troops, and he-- and they, they patched us through and asked the troops to, to clear off a section of the road so we could land-- 600 feet minimum.
And they put panels down, which was no problem.
So we went to-- from Munich to Dachau, and then the road was probably a mile or so away from the camp, so we flew over the camp.
And it looked like a riot.
We could-- you know, and we couldn't hear anything.
But we landed.
I think there were about five airplanes.
And we had one airplane that was radio-- it was just a radio airplane, no passengers, and so we-- he was the one who could talk to Frankfurt and tell us what was going on.
And anyhow, we landed.
We were met by command cars again.
Our, our passengers-- these were different passengers-- disappeared with some generals into the ga-- in the command cars.
And then, again, I hooked a ride with a guy in a jeep and called him by name and told him who I was, blah, blah, blah.
And again, we're driving up the road.
This is in Bavaria now.
This is really beautiful country and beautiful little homes with the flowers-- this was April-- the flowers.
We have them out in our front yard every year.
I can't think of them right now but-- geraniums.
Everybody had geraniums growing there-- all in their window sills and just beautiful.
But here again, we get closer, and I start to smell this and I said, my god, are they all like this?
And he said, all?
Oh, you mean there's more?
I said, yeah, we just were in Buchenwald at Weimar a couple, three days ago.
He said, Jesus.
Typical GI, bad words.
And here again, the entrance was the same, the gate was the same.
There were two-- actually two gates.
On both sides was the-- were the guard towers-- I mean the guard-- brick towers.
Yeah, the, the usual, 'Arbeit macht frei.' And there was-- there was one of them that had something else.
And I'll tell-- I'll think of that in a minute.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had-- they had that, but it had-- and this was all done in wrought iron by the inmates.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK.
Before we got-- went through the gates, he said, this, this is-- this is going to blow you away or something to that effect.
I mean, and there was a train that was probably 60, 80 cars.
I don't know how.
And it had been-- it was on a side track, and it was-- he drove us down the railroad track, me and another guy.
And it was, you know, kind of like this.
But he said, look in those cars.
And it was filled with dead-- with dead people.
I mean every car.
Most of them were naked.
Some of them had been shot.
There were medics, guys that had the Red Cross on their helmets and on their shoulders, going through these bodies.
And I guess they found a few, I don't know, alive.
There were bodies alongside the track, which had been shot, men had-- men who had been shot.
You could see the blood.
But then there were also bodies along there that there were no blood.
So we spent about 30 minutes going up one side, and I got out and looked in some of the cars.
And, and I'd had no idea what the hell was going on, except this train was full of dead people.
And then he said, I'll show you where the firefight was.
And I said, OK.
So he went to back toward the gate, and we went through the gate.
Now there was guards on the gate who were ordered not to let anybody in or anybody out.
When we got to the gate, I told the guard I was with [INAUDIBLE] and I had a [INAUDIBLE] pass.
And he said, well, I'm sorry, sir.
Nobody in, nobody out.
I said, well, you better call your CO because I have people in there.
So he did.
And he let me in, and this-- me and this other guy and the jeep driver.
So he took us to the towers, which was about every 50, 100 feet, let's say, wooden towers, all around the perimeter of the camp.

And at about-- I'm going to guess-- 30%, 40% of the towers, there were SS troopers on the ground of the tow-- on the bottom of the towers, say 10 or 12 in each tower-- not every tower, but, but say 20% of the towers.
And he said, this is where they had the big showdown with the 45th.
And I said, the hell you say.
What do you mean?
He said, well, the scouts came in the camp.
The towers-- the, the-- one German officer and a Red Cross guy tried to surrender-- this was what they-- he told me, and some of this is in the book, some of it isn't.
These guys, these SS, decided to hell with them.
We're going to have a shoot out.
And he fired-- somebody fired on these Americans.
And these guys had been in battle since Anzio.
They are not to be trifled with, as I learned later.
They were hard ass infantry dog faces who had been from Anzio to Dachau-- casualty rates of over 50%.
And, and this was the 45th Division that I had been in in Fort Sill, Oklahoma, remember? 45th Thunderbird Division.
And saw the shoulder patches and the helmets that they have on some of them have decals on the helmet.
I thought, Jesus, I've died and gone to heaven.
Here I'm with the 45th again.
So what had happened, as was explained to me, that this-- that these-- they had a firefight.
The infantry did their thing, which is hide and shoot back.
Some of the SS surr-- tried to surrender.
And they said, throw your arms down and come down out of the towers.
And they did, and they just shot them.
These infantry Americans just-- they probably were 30, 40, I don't know.
But they-- some of them still had their weapons.
They were still laying there, you know, but they-- but they were all-- they were all killed with machine guns.
And so that was that.
And then there was a moat where there were some SS bodies that had been-- evidently been thrown in the moat.
Now this is the day of liberation.
And we're still not in the main part of the camp yet.
We had just gone in to the-- to the train, which was not inside the camp.
It was on the perimeters-- and the towers, which we could get to from the outside of the camp.
I mean, they were partly in and partly out.
You know what I'm saying?
We went back to the guar-- to the gates, and the guy wouldn't let us in.
We're going to stop this tape.
OK.
